metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify how WS3 converts the `applies_to` globs in `criteria_routing.json` into Round-A boolean flags: if the existing `overlay_triggers` step does not support file-glob inputs (it doesn't — only keyword/structural), specify whether WS3 adds that capability, or whether WS2's `applies_to` globs are read via `select_reviewers()`/`load_catalog()` from the prompt front-matter instead (which is already implemented), and update the plan to name the actual mechanism rather than claiming it is 'EXISTING'.
findings.finding: The plan claims 'WS3's existing overlay_triggers op reads these globs (from criteria_routing.json) to emit the deterministic Round-A include_<overlay> flags — the EXISTING glob mechanism, not a new one.' But the existing overlay_triggers workflow step (workflow/steps.py) only supports keyword_triggers (substring matching against text) and structural (ticket-graph) triggers — it has no file-glob-matching capability against a changed-files list. File-glob-based reviewer selection in the codebase is performed by prompts.select_reviewers() + _glob_match() reading from reviewers/index.json (category:review entries), NOT from criteria_routing.json. This means either WS3 must add a new glob-matching trigger kind to overlay_triggers (contradicting 'EXISTING'), or the deterministic triggering path works differently than described — leaving the mechanism for converting applies_to globs in criteria_routing.json into Round-A include_<overlay> booleans unspecified.
findings.location: Overlay `applies_to` globs section / 'WS3's existing overlay_triggers op reads these globs (from criteria_routing.json)'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'WS3's existing overlay_triggers op reads these globs (from criteria_routing.json)' with: 'WS3 wires deterministic glob-triggering via prompts.select_reviewers(changed_files) — the existing mechanism that already reads applies_to globs from the prompt front-matter — and emits include_<overlay> booleans from that result; the applies_to globs in criteria_routing.json serve the threshold_for() lookup only and do not need to be re-read by the workflow step.' This eliminates the duplicate-source problem and uses the already-implemented mechanism.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that WS3's `overlay_triggers` op already reads `applies_to` globs from `criteria_routing.json` (or explicitly scope that mechanism as a WS3 deliverable, not an existing one), so the WS2→WS3 interface contract is grounded.
findings.finding: The plan asserts that WS3 already has 'the EXISTING glob mechanism' — an overlay_triggers op that reads applies_to globs from criteria_routing.json — and that WS2 merely needs to populate that field for WS3 to consume. The codebase shows the actual overlay_triggers in plan_review/registry.py reads from an inline _DET_OVERLAY_RULES regex dict, not from criteria_routing.json (which has no glob field). A code-review criteria_routing.json can add an applies_to field, but the 'existing' glob-reading mechanism that WS3 would consume from it is unverified.
findings.location: ## Overlay `applies_to` globs section — last paragraph: 'WS3's existing `overlay_triggers` op reads these globs (from `criteria_routing.json`) to emit the deterministic Round-A `include_<overlay>` flags — the EXISTING glob mechanism, not a new one.'
findings.suggested_fix: If the glob-reading mechanism is a WS3 new build (not pre-existing), state that explicitly: 'WS3 will add an overlay_triggers op that reads applies_to globs from code_review/criteria_routing.json' — removing the 'EXISTING mechanism' claim that the codebase does not support.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly assign ownership of converting `src/rebar/llm/code_review.py` → `src/rebar/llm/code_review/__init__.py` to WS1 or WS2, and confirm it precedes the WS2 work that creates sibling modules under `src/rebar/llm/code_review/`.
findings.finding: The plan creates `src/rebar/llm/code_review/registry.py`, `moves.py`, `contracts.py`, and `criteria_routing.json` as a new Python package, but today `code_review.py` is a flat module at `src/rebar/llm/code_review.py`. Python's `importlib.resources` (used by the plan_review precedent to load `criteria_routing.json`) requires the JSON to live inside a proper package (`rebar.llm.code_review`). Creating `code_review/registry.py` alongside the flat `code_review.py` is a file-system conflict — both cannot coexist; the flat module must be converted to `code_review/__init__.py` first. The plan does not mention this conversion step or assign ownership to WS1/WS2.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Prerequisites / WS1 handoff' bullet: 'WS1 converts the flat `code_review.py` to `code_review/__init__.py` (preserving `__all__` and public API) so WS2 can add sibling modules (`registry.py`, `moves.py`, `contracts.py`) under the same package and use `importlib.resources.files("rebar.llm.code_review")` to load `criteria_routing.json`.' Alternatively assign the conversion to WS2 as its first step.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the WS2 plan how `criteria_routing.json` globs reach the `overlay_triggers` workflow step: either (a) WS3 manually copies/references the globs into the YAML `keyword_triggers`, or (b) a new `overlay_triggers` variant that reads the JSON is introduced (and scope that work to WS2 or WS3).
findings.finding: The plan states 'WS3's existing `overlay_triggers` op reads these globs (from `criteria_routing.json`) to emit the deterministic Round-A `include_<overlay>` flags — the EXISTING glob mechanism, not a new one.' But the shipped `overlay_triggers` step (workflow/steps.py lines 115–170) reads `keyword_triggers` from its YAML `with:` block — it does NOT read `criteria_routing.json` at runtime. The `criteria_routing.json` globs would have to be wired into the workflow YAML's `keyword_triggers` at gate-authoring time (WS3), or a new step variant that reads the JSON directly would be needed. The plan's characterisation of this as 'the EXISTING glob mechanism' overstates what exists.
findings.location: Overlay `applies_to` globs section — 'WS3's existing overlay_triggers op reads these globs (from criteria_routing.json)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify how `applies_to` globs in `criteria_routing.json` are actually matched against a diff's changed files at gate time: the existing `overlay_triggers` step has no `changed_files`/glob input — either (a) name the existing code path that will read the globs (e.g. `select_code_reviewers()` already does this), (b) specify a new `glob_triggers` input to `overlay_triggers` as a WS3 deliverable, or (c) update the plan to say WS3 adds this capability and WS2 only populates the globs in the JSON.
findings.finding: The plan claims 'WS3's existing overlay_triggers op reads these globs (from criteria_routing.json) to emit the deterministic Round-A include_<overlay> flags — the EXISTING glob mechanism, not a new one.' Tool inspection of the overlay_triggers step (workflow/steps.py:133-173) and its input schema (overlay_triggers_input.schema.json) confirms this step has NO file-glob or changed_files input — it supports only keyword_triggers (substring text match), structural, and linked_types. There is no existing mechanism for matching applies_to globs against a diff's changed files in this step. The referenced protocol does not exist in the codebase.
findings.location: Plan section: 'Overlay applies_to globs' — 'WS3's existing overlay_triggers op reads these globs…'
findings.suggested_fix: The actual glob-matching machinery already exists in `prompts.select_reviewers()` / `code_review.select_code_reviewers()` (which reads `applies_to` from index.json). WS2 should specify that the `include_<overlay>` flags are derived from `select_code_reviewers(changed_files)` at gate-assembly time (a WS3 concern), and the `applies_to` globs in `criteria_routing.json` are the SOURCE for the prompt-index, not an input to the `overlay_triggers` step directly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Designate a single source of truth for `applies_to` globs: either the prompt front-matter only (with WS3 reading them via `select_reviewers()`/`load_catalog()`, matching the existing pattern), or `criteria_routing.json` only (with the front-matter field omitted or marked informational) — and remove the 'mirrors them' language that implies both are authoritative.
findings.finding: The plan specifies that applies_to globs live in BOTH the prompt front-matter (each code-review-<overlay>.md) AND criteria_routing.json (which 'mirrors them'). This creates two sources of truth for the same data with no drift-prevention mechanism specified. The existing system avoids this: plan_review/criteria_routing.json does not duplicate data from prompt front-matter (applies_to is absent from plan_review's criteria_routing.json entries). If the two sources drift — a prompt's applies_to is updated but criteria_routing.json is not, or vice versa — the behavioral effect depends on which source WS3 reads, and there is no test or regeneration step to detect the divergence.

findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the module (e.g. a new `code_review/passes.py` or an extension to `workflow_ops.py`) that assembles `active_triggers` for the code-review coach, specify the derivation rule (e.g. 'the set of overlay-ids from surviving findings' `criteria` field vs a separate `overlay_id` field), and add this to the WS2 Scope or AC7.
findings.finding: The rule 'the gate passes active_triggers = the set of overlay-ids that produced surviving findings' (Move-catalog section) is defined far from the module that must implement it, and no WS2 file is named as the owner of this assembly logic. In plan-review, this is implemented in passes.py:538 as `triggers = {c for f in surviving for c in f.get('criteria', [])}` — but for code-review the derivation is semantically different (overlay-ids from findings, not criterion-ids), and neither the `code-review-coach` prompt AC nor the contracts.py spec names a module or function that computes active_triggers before calling coach().
findings.location: Plan section: 'Move-catalog — concrete starter moves + applies_when vocabulary' and AC7 ('a test asserts the kernel coach() renders a picked move's template deterministically')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit sequencing note: consider shipping the `threshold_for` resolver + `criteria_routing.json` + registry extension (the WS3-blocking interface) as a thin first slice, with the 11 overlay prompts and move-catalog following — this de-risks the critical path before the mechanical authoring work begins.
findings.finding: WS2 delivers ~18 files across 4 layers (LLM prompts, Python registry/contracts, JSON config, docs) with no proposed thin vertical slice or evidence-gated MVP, creating a horizontal big-bang risk. The riskiest piece — `threshold_for` + `criteria_routing.json`, on which WS3 depends — is delivered in the same batch as 11 mechanical overlay prompts. If the routing interface needs revision after WS3 integration, all 18 files must be revisited together.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Sequencing' subsection: Phase 1 (unblocks WS3): criteria_routing.json + threshold_for in registry.py + the test validating the schema and behavior (AC4, AC5, AC6). Phase 2 (can overlap or follow): the 11 overlay finder prompts + verify preamble (AC1, AC2). Phase 3: moves.py + coach prompt + contracts extension (AC3, AC7). Phase 1 is the thin vertical slice that proves the interface contract before any prompt authoring happens.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend AC7) asserting that the kernel coach() filters moves by active_triggers: e.g., a move with applies_when=['security'] is NOT returned when active_triggers does not include 'security', and IS returned when it does.
findings.finding: The applies_when filtering behavior — that the kernel coach() only offers moves whose applies_when overlaps active_triggers — is described in the plan body but no acceptance criterion tests it; AC3 only validates the vocabulary constraint, and AC7 only tests template rendering.
findings.location: Move-catalog section ('the gate passes active_triggers = the set of overlay-ids that produced surviving findings'); AC3; AC7

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the module path and WS that defines `VERIFIER_RULES_SCAFFOLD` (e.g., `code_review/scaffolds.py` authored in WS1), or add its full text inline in the verify-prompt spec.
findings.finding: The verify prompt AC says `code-review-verify.md` must 'embed `VERIFIER_RULES_SCAFFOLD`', but the plan never states where `VERIFIER_RULES_SCAFFOLD` is defined, what it contains, or which WS/module owns it. An executing agent cannot locate the artifact to embed.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-line note to the Scope section: "`VERIFIER_RULES_SCAFFOLD` is defined in `src/rebar/llm/code_review/scaffolds.py` (WS1 artifact); `code-review-verify.md` imports/references it via the prompt-loader's `{% include %}` mechanism." Adjust if the actual location differs.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify which WS owns the kernel `coach()` function and either add it to WS2's Scope (if WS2 must author it) or reframe AC 7 as an integration test gated on that WS's completion.
findings.finding: AC bullet 7 requires a test that 'asserts the kernel `coach()` renders a picked move's template deterministically', but WS2's Scope section does not include authoring the kernel `coach()` function. If `coach()` is a WS1 or WS3 artifact not yet built when WS2 executes, this test cannot pass within WS2's session.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC bullet 7; Scope section; 'Coach prompt output contract' section
findings.suggested_fix: If the kernel is a WS1 artifact, add '`coach()` is defined in WS1's kernel module; WS2 adds an integration test asserting template substitution against the live function.' If WS3 owns it, reword AC 7 to: 'WS2 authors a unit test stub with a local `coach()` stand-in; the full integration test is gated on WS3.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify ownership of VERIFIER_RULES_SCAFFOLD: if WS1-owned, add an explicit dependency statement (as done for OVERLAY_IDS); if WS2-owned, add a Scope entry and a covering AC asserting it is defined and referenced correctly.
findings.finding: VERIFIER_RULES_SCAFFOLD is referenced in the What section and AC2 as something the verify preamble 'embeds,' but the plan never identifies whether it is a WS1-owned artifact (dependency) or a WS2 deliverable — no Scope entry, no AC, and no origin statement covers it.
findings.location: What section ('A code-review verify-prompt preamble that EMBEDS VERIFIER_RULES_SCAFFOLD'); AC2 ('code-review-verify.md embeds VERIFIER_RULES_SCAFFOLD')
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence under the Scope or What section: 'VERIFIER_RULES_SCAFFOLD is defined in WS1's code_review/registry.py (or verifier.py) and imported by code-review-verify.md at render time' — mirroring how OVERLAY_IDS ownership is stated.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend AC3) to require a test asserting that `validate_move_registry` raises (or otherwise rejects) a move whose `applies_when` contains a tag outside `{OVERLAY_IDS ∪ 'always'}`, confirming the closed-vocabulary invariant is enforced at load.
findings.finding: The test plan covers happy-path validation of `validate_move_registry` (AC3: 'validates against MOVE_REGISTRY_SCHEMA at load') but specifies no test for the rejection path — i.e., what happens when a move carries an `applies_when` tag outside the closed `{OVERLAY_IDS ∪ 'always'}` vocabulary. Without a rejection test, the closed-vocabulary guarantee is asserted by the plan but unverified by the suite.
findings.location: AC3 / ## Move-catalog — concrete starter moves + applies_when vocabulary
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC3: 'a test asserts validate_move_registry raises ValueError (or equivalent) when a move carries an applies_when tag not in OVERLAY_IDS ∪ {"always"}'.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
